pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Quickstart completion improved from 44% to 58% after the docs copy refresh, but the biggest remaining drop is during local setup and credential configuration due to environment-specific errors.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly states completion rose from 44% to 58% after the copy refresh and that the biggest remaining drop is local setup and credential configuration with environment-specific errors.

claimJudgments.claimText: Support volume is dominated by setup failures (environment drift, stale CLI versions, missing environment variables, and credentials from the wrong workspace), and users often need an internal recovery checklist that is not in public docs—leading some teams to re-run the quickstart and reproduce the failed state.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says most new tickets mention environment drift, stale CLI versions, and unclear recovery steps; it groups tickets into stale CLI installs, missing environment variables, and wrong-workspace credentials. It also says an internal checklist resolved most cases but is not reflected in public docs, and several tickets reopened after users repeated the quickstart and reproduced the failed state.

claimJudgments.claimText: Pilot expansion is considered risky until failed-install/setup recovery is visibly demonstrable with documented guidance; the launch owner flagged setup recovery as the failure that would make demos feel brittle.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 states two additional teams are available but the launch owner flagged setup recovery as the visible failure that would make the demo feel brittle, and recommends expansion only after failed-install guidance and citation checks are observable in the lab.

claimJudgments.claimText: Pilot readiness gates explicitly block scheduling more teams until (1) public failed-install recovery steps exist and (2) generated recommendations show source citations and expose whether they came from analytics, support, or interviews.
claimJudgments.explanation: S6 lists three gates for adding teams: public failed-install recovery steps, source citations on generated recommendations, and provenance by analytics/support/interviews. It also says two teams are ready but scheduling is blocked until setup recovery can be demonstrated without support using an internal checklist.

claimJudgments.claimText: Pilot users’ trust in generated recommendations depends on an inspectable evidence trail (source snippets/links) and clear separation of evidence types (analytics vs support vs interviews), more than prose quality.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly says senior engineers trust generated recommendations when claims point back to inspectable source snippets, are less concerned about prose polish, and value separating analytics, support, and interview evidence rather than blending it.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The briefing answers the task by summarizing adoption risks around setup/credential recovery and trust in cited recommendations, then recommends the most supported next product-quality improvement: publish and validate a self-serve setup recovery path, with citation/provenance visibility handled in parallel for readiness gates. The recommendation is well grounded in S2, S4, and S6, with S1 and S3 supporting the risk framing. It does not materially overstate pilot cancellation; it frames expansion as gated or waiting until prerequisites are met.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S4 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S6 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S3 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
